input_candidates.truth_value: The studied steel was produced by vacuum induction melting, cast into an ingot, forged at 1050°C and homogenized for 2 h at 1050°C before reheating and hot rolling.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Figure 5 (tabular XRD data) reports austenite fractions before and after deformation: AC-A800 58.6%→23.7%, AC-A900 61.4%→44.4%, AC-A1000 57.1%→46.4%, FC-A800 62%→56.5%, FC-A900 58.6%→52.4%, FC-A1000 60.6%→52.1%, indicating pronounced TRIP effect in air-cooled annealed samples.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fracture surface of AC-A800 showed dimples indicating high ductility, while FC-A800 exhibited cracking at martensite–austenite interfaces and cleavage-dominated brittle fracture.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Figure 9 provides tabular engineering stress–strain data for all hot-rolled and annealed samples; air-cooled annealed samples achieve elongations of 56.9%, 39.5%, and 31.1%, whereas furnace-cooled annealed samples achieve only 19.0%, 15.4%, and 10.6%.

input_candidates.truth_value: During annealing of air-cooled samples, carbon and manganese partition into austenite, increasing chemical stability; needle-like α-ferrite further refines austenite, enhancing mechanical stability.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Figure 10 provides tabular true stress–strain and work hardening rate data, showing an extra Stage III work hardening rate recovery in air-cooled annealed samples and abrupt fracture without necking in furnace-cooled annealed samples.

input_candidates.truth_value: In furnace-cooled annealed samples, cracks are easily nucleated at the martensite–austenite interface, and delamination of banded microstructure leads to brittle fracture.
input_candidates.prediction_value: The low-angle grain boundary lengths in ferrite measured by EBSD are 4351.1 μm (AC-A800), 2685.4 μm (AC-A900), and 1634.3 μm (AC-A1000), indicating progressive recovery during annealing.

input_candidates.truth_value: XRD measurements of austenite fraction after deformation: AC-A800 23.7%, AC-A900 44.4%, AC-A1000 46.4%; FC-A800 56.5%, FC-A900 52.4%, FC-A1000 52.1%.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fracture surface of AC-A800 exhibits dimples indicative of ductile fracture, while FC-A800 exhibits cleavage-dominated brittle fracture with cracking at martensite–austenite interfaces and delamination of banded microstructure.

input_candidates.truth_value: The KAM values of austenite in hot-rolled AC and FC samples are 0.48° and 0.43°, respectively, indicating higher dislocation density in AC due to martensitic transformation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: The austenite grain size distributions in Figure 7 show that furnace-cooled annealed samples have much larger austenite grains than air-cooled annealed samples at all annealing temperatures.

candidates.truth_value: The studied steel was produced by vacuum induction melting, cast into an ingot, forged at 1050°C and homogenized for 2 h at 1050°C before reheating and hot rolling.
